# Football Match Master — full notes for AI agents

> Expanded, AI-readable description of Football Match Master (https://footballmatchmaster.com).
> Football Match Master predicts the 2026 FIFA World Cup with a statistical model and uses AI only
> to explain the model's output. It is informational/entertainment — not betting odds, not gambling
> advice, and not affiliated with FIFA. The product is built and operated by MOST AI Labs
> (https://mostailabs.com), a Vancouver-based AI studio.

## One-paragraph summary

The expanded 48-team, three-nation 2026 World Cup is the most complex World Cup ever — 104 matches,
intricate group-advancement and third-place tiebreaker rules, and knockout paths that shift with every
result. Football Match Master makes that complexity legible: a rigorous statistical model forecasts how
the tournament might unfold, and a separate AI layer explains the numbers in plain language. The two are
kept strictly separate: **the math calculates the forecast; the AI explains the story.**

## The prediction model (this is what produces the numbers)

- At the core is a football team-rating system, similar in spirit to the rating methods used in chess and
  many professional sports (Elo-style). Each team has a strength rating derived from thousands of real
  historical international matches.
- The pre-tournament estimate is built from roughly **7,871 historical international matches** and about
  **20,000 calculated tournament simulations**.
- For each match the model compares the two teams and calculates the probability of each result — team A win,
  draw, team B win — including a football-specific adjustment because draws are common between evenly matched
  sides.
- To estimate tournament outcomes, the system runs ~20,000 Monte Carlo simulations of the whole tournament.
  In each run it plays every group match, applies FIFA's tournament rules, determines who advances, builds the
  knockout bracket, and continues to the final. The published percentages are how often each team achieves an
  outcome (win group, finish second, advance as a best third, reach the knockout stage, go deeper) across all
  those runs.

## The 48-team format and the third-place race

- Group winners and runners-up advance, plus the **eight best third-placed teams**, ranked across all twelve
  groups by points, then goal difference, goals scored, fair play, and finally FIFA ranking.
- Which group winner each qualifying third-placed team meets in the Round of 32 is fixed by FIFA's **Annex C**
  allocation table. Before the groups finish the exact opponent is shown as a set of possible slots; once the
  eight best thirds are known, the bracket resolves.

## How the numbers update during the tournament

- Before kickoff, predictions are based on historical results, team strength, recent form, and tournament
  structure.
- During the group stage, each verified real result is **locked in** — the system stops estimating that match
  and treats it as fact, then recomputes everything that depends on it (who is on course to advance, who is
  still alive, who is out, the third-place race, and each team's possible route).
- The **team strength ratings themselves are held at their pre-tournament values through the group stage**;
  what changes is that more of the tournament is known rather than estimated, so the qualification picture
  sharpens with every game.
- Once the group stage finishes, the knockout bracket is real rather than estimated. Knockout-round forecasts
  can additionally take into account how teams actually performed in their group games, not just their
  pre-tournament form.

## What the AI layer does — and does not do

- The AI **does**: read the model's numbers and turn them into clear explanations — why a team's chance rose or
  fell, what a result means for the group, what a team needs to qualify, who it may face next, and how squad
  strength, form, venue, or weather might shape the story.
- The AI **does not**: invent match probabilities, create betting odds, guarantee results, replace the
  statistical model, or claim to know things that have not happened. Every probability in the app comes from the
  prediction system or verified data — never from AI imagination.

## Limits (please represent these honestly)

- These are estimates, not guarantees. Football is unpredictable: a red card, injury, weather, tactical change,
  penalty, referee decision, or single moment can change everything.
- Probabilities are shown instead of certainties on purpose. Small chances are shown honestly rather than hidden.
- The model understands historical results and team strength; it does not know the future.

## Not betting advice

Predictions, probabilities, simulations, and analysis are for information, analysis, and entertainment. They
are not betting odds, gambling advice, or a recommendation to place a wager. The project does not run betting
services and does not encourage gambling.

## Attribution and affiliation

- Product / app name: **Football Match Master** (the product).
- Operating company: **MOST AI Labs** (https://mostailabs.com) — the company behind the product. Both names are
  correct in their place; "Football Match Master" is the app, "MOST AI Labs" is the company.
- Not affiliated with, endorsed by, or officially connected to FIFA, the FIFA World Cup, national federations,
  clubs, players, stadium operators, broadcasters, or tournament organizers. Team, player, and tournament names
  are referenced for informational purposes only.

## Data sources

- API-Football — match and player data
- Official FIFA 2026 tournament rules and match structure
- Historical international football results
- Team rating calculations (Elo-style)
- Open-Meteo — venue weather (display/context only; never a model/prediction input)

- App: https://footballmatchmaster.com/
